coredata.dc:title: Azithromycin detection in cells and tablets by N,S co-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Xueqing
Liu, Yang
Dong, Wenjuan
Hu, Qin
Li, Yong
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
Cai, Lishuai
Gong, Xiaojuan
coredata.dc:description:
Azithromycin (AZM) 1
1 AZM: azithromycin. is one of the most widely used antibiotics. AZM abuse is easy to cause great harm to human body, so developing a rapid and sensitive method to detect AZM is of great importance. Herein, 3-aminothiophenol as only reaction precursor, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) 2
2 N,S-CQDs: nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots. were fabricated by one-step hydrothermal carbonization method. All characteristics demonstrate that N,S-CQDs possess good water solubility, high fluorescence stability and low cytotoxicity. Without being disturbed by amino acids and drugs, the most interesting finding is that AZM can efficiently quench the fluorescence of N,S-CQDs by a synergistic effect of electrostatic interaction and static quenching. A fluorescent probe for the detection of AZM was constructed with high selectivity and good sensitivity, achieving two linear ranges of 2.5–32.3 μM and 37.2–110 μM and a limit of detection of 0.76 µM. The proposed fluorescent method was used for the detection of AZM in cells with fulfilling results. More importantly, the fluorescent probe was successfully used to the detection of AZM in tablets and human urine with recovery rate and relative standard deviations of 98.2–104.8% and 0.04–3.46%, respectively, which was confirmed by the standard method of HPLC-UV. This finding illustrates the usefulness and feasibility of N,S-CQDs as an effective fluorescent probe for the detection of AZM in tablets and human urine, which is helpful for supervising and guiding pharmacy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Azithromycin
N,S co-doped carbon quantum dots
Cells
Tablets

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) measurement of N,S-CQDs
Cellular toxicity experiment and cellular imaging
Preparation of samples and calibration curves
Sensitive determination of AZM by N,S-CQDs and mechanism research
Qualitative analysis of AZM in living cells by N,S-CQDs
Quantitative determination of AZM in dosage forms by N,S-CQDs

Sincerely thank Dr. Ying Zuo of the Scientific Instrument Centre at Shanxi University for taking the AFM images.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The representative (A) AFM and (B) TEM (Inset: the enlarged TEM image (top left corner) and the size distribution diagram of N,S-CQDs (bottom left corner)) images of N,S-CQDs.
(A) XPS survey scan, (B) C1s XPS, (C) N1s XPS, (D) S2p XPS, (E) O1s XPS and (F) FTIR spectrum of N,S-CQDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption (red line), fluorescence excitation (blue line) and emission (black line) spectra of N,S-CQDs (Inset: shooting figures of N,S-CQDs under natural light and ultraviolet light and the enlarged UV–vis absorption image in the range of 430 nm and 520 nm). (B) The emission spectra of N,S-CQDs under different excitation wavelength (350–510 nm).

(A) The fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs in the absence and presence of AZM under ultrapure water and different pHs. (B) The fluorescence spectra of N,S-CQDs in the presence of AZM at various concentrations (0–474 μM) from top to bottom (Inset: the trend chart of the whole titration process). The linear relation between N,S-CQDs and AZM in the range of (C) 2.5–32.3 μM and (D) 37.2–110 μM.

(A) The zeta potentials of N,S-CQDs, AZM and the mixture of N,S-CQDs and AZM. (B) Fluorescence decay curves of N,S-CQDs (0.1 mg/mL) in the absence and presence of different concentration (2.5, 7.5, 12.5, 22.4, 37, 52 and 67 μM) of AZM.

(A) The cell viability of A549 and PC12 under different concentrations of N,S-CQDs (0, 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 μg/mL). (B) LSCM images of A549 and PC12 cells incubated with N,S-CQDs (100 µg/mL) without and with AZM (0.1 mM, 0.2 mM or 0.4 mM) at 37 °C for 8 h. The first and third panels are bright-field images. The second and fifth panels are cell images taken at λex/λem of 488 nm/490–650 nm. The third and sixth panels are the merged images of first and second panel and fourth and fifth panel, respectively.
The diagram illustrates the preparation process of N,S-CQDs.
Comparison of different analytical methods for the detection of AZM.

row.entry:
0–2.0 μM in plasma and blood
0–12 μM in water

56.1 nM ± 22.7 nM in plasma
159 nM ± 86.8 nM in blood
96.1 nM ± 48.1 nM in water

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aHPLC-PCD-FLD: high-performance liquid chromatographic method with pre-column derivatization on and fluorescence detection.
bLLLME-LC: liquid-liquid-liquid microextraction technique combined with liquid chromatography.
cHPLC-PDA: high-performance liquid chromatography combined with photodiode array detector.
dLC-MS/MS: liquid chromatographic-tandem mass spectrometric method.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The detection results of AZM in commercial drug and human urine by N,S-CQDs-based fluorescent probe (n = 3, µM). (B) Analytical results of AZM in commercial drug and human urine by N,S-CQDs-based fluorescent probe.

head.ce:title: Azithromycin detection in cells and tablets by N,S co-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Azithromycin (AZM) 1
1 AZM: azithromycin. is one of the most widely used antibiotics. AZM abuse is easy to cause great harm to human body, so developing a rapid and sensitive method to detect AZM is of great importance. Herein, 3-aminothiophenol as only reaction precursor, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) 2
2 N,S-CQDs: nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots. were fabricated by one-step hydrothermal carbonization method. All characteristics demonstrate that N,S-CQDs possess good water solubility, high fluorescence stability and low cytotoxicity. Without being disturbed by amino acids and drugs, the most interesting finding is that AZM can efficiently quench the fluorescence of N,S-CQDs by a synergistic effect of electrostatic interaction and static quenching. A fluorescent probe for the detection of AZM was constructed with high selectivity and good sensitivity, achieving two linear ranges of 2.5–32.3 μM and 37.2–110 μM and a limit of detection of 0.76 µM. The proposed fluorescent method was used for the detection of AZM in cells with fulfilling results. More importantly, the fluorescent probe was successfully used to the detection of AZM in tablets and human urine with recovery rate and relative standard deviations of 98.2–104.8% and 0.04–3.46%, respectively, which was confirmed by the standard method of HPLC-UV. This finding illustrates the usefulness and feasibility of N,S-CQDs as an effective fluorescent probe for the detection of AZM in tablets and human urine, which is helpful for supervising and guiding pharmacy.

ce:section.ce:para: Azithromycin (AZM) is a fifteen-membered macrolide and broad-spectrum antibiotics, which is a semi-synthetic erythromycin derivative with a methyl-substituted nitrogen atom at the 9a position of the lactone ring [1,2]. AZM can effectively prevent a variety of bacteria such as gram-positive bacterium, gram-negative bacterium, mycoplasma, chlamydia and other typical pathogens by inhibiting the synthesis process of bacterial protein and is widely used in clinic [1,2]. Hence, using correctly and detecting precisely of AZM is of great significance. High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with a UV detector has been rapidly gained acceptance as an analytical technique for AZM analysis [3,4], whereas the weak UV absorption peak at around 210 nm of AZM brings about asymmetric peak shape and low column efficiency. Other methods including electrochemical method [5,6], pre-column derivatization fluorescent method [7,8], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [1] and liquid-liquid-liquid microextraction (LLLME) technology [9] have been developed for the detection of AZM in conventional pharmaceutical dosage forms or biological matrices. But these approaches have some drawbacks such as complicated pre-processing steps, expensive instruments, low sensitivity and long time-consuming. In consequence, we need to exploit a simple and cost-effective method to sensitively and efficiently determinate AZM.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new-type of quasi-zero dimensional carbon nanomaterial with spherical structure and contains abundant surface functional groups [10,11]. CQDs possess a lot of advantages such as excellent fluorescence, easy functionalization and good biocompatibility [12], so CQDs have aroused tremendous research interest and regarded as one of the most promising nanomaterials for fluorescence probes/sensors [13–19], photothermal therapy [20], bioimaging [10,20] and drug delivery [21,22]. Heteroatom doping into CQDs is an available approach to elevate the performance of CQDs, while most of the reported doped-CQDs are fabricated by two or more reaction precursors [23,24], which may increase the cost of preparation and the difficulty of purification. Thus, it is still a challenge to develop a method to synthesize two kinds of heteroatoms-doped CQDs by a facile and single precursor. We want to synthesize a novel CQDs doped with nitrogen and sulfur by the only precursor, for further enriching the types and applications of CQDs.

In this work, 3-aminothiophenol as the reaction precursor, nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) is produced by ultrafast hydrothermal process (Scheme 1 ). All characterization results show that N,S-CQDs possess excellent water-solubility, narrow size distribution, bright green fluorescence (quantum yield: 11.96%) and ultra-low cytotoxicity. AZM can effectively quench the green fluorescence of N,S-CQDs, so we construct a sensitivity fluorescent probe to determinate AZM. More importantly, we successfully determine AZM in cells, human urine and actual tablets.

3-Aminothiophenol (C6H7NS), azithromycin (AZM, 749 g/mol, 98%), aspartic acid (Asp, 98%), glutamic acid (Glu, 99%), threonine (Thr, 99%), valine (Val, 99%), tyrosine (Tyr, 98%), proline (Pro, 99%), glutamine (Gln, 99%), phenylalanine (Phe, 98%), asparagine (Asn, 98%), cysteine (Cys, 97%), alanine (Ala, 99%), isoleucine (Ile, 99%), leucine (Leu, 99%), glycine (Gly, 99%), serine (Ser, 99%), tryptophan (Trp, 99%) methionine (Met, 99%), vitamin B1 (VB1, 98%), riboflavin (VB2, 98%), niacin (VB3, 99%), vitamin H (VB7, 98%), folic acid (VB9, 97%), thiamphenicol (THI, 99%), clarithromycin (CLA, 98%), gentamicin (GEN, 59%), doxorubicin (DOX, 98%), vancomycin (VAN, 90%), lincomycin (LIN, 95%), sulfadiazine (SD, 98%), tinidazole (TIN, >98%), metronidazole (MET, 99%), ornidazole (ORN, 98%), amoxicillin (AMX, 98%), ursolic acid (UA, 98%), penicillamine (PEN, 98%), ammonium citrate (AC, 98.5%), cinnamic aldehyde (CA, ≥95%), penicillin G sodium (PG, 1650 μg/mg), cefradine (CH, 97%) and morin (MH, >90%) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. Absolute ethanol (C2H5OH, EtOH), methanol (MeOH), KH2PO4 and NaOH were provided by J&K Scientific Ltd. Dulbecco’s phosphate buffered saline (DPBS) and Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) were purchased from ThermoFisher Scientific Ltd. All chemicals were not further purified and used as received directly. Milli-Q ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ·cm was employed throughout the experiment.

N,S-CQDs are fabricated by a simple hydrothermal using 3-aminothiophenol as the precursor. Specifically, 0.07 g of 3-aminothiophenol was completely dissolved in 10 mL EtOH without any undissolved substance, and then the mixture was totally injected into a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was kept in 180 °C for 12 h, and then naturally cooled to ambient temperature. The obtained the chocolate solution was removed EtOH by a rotary evaporator and then was re-dissolved into ultrapure water to acquire the orange-yellow solution. Subsequently, after the solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and filtered with a syringe filter (pore size of 0.22 μm) to remove larger particulate impurities, the supernatant was collected and dialyzed in dialysis tube with MWCO of 1000 Da for 6 h against ultrapure water with re-changing the ultrapure water every 3 h. The dialyzed solution was lyophilized to acquire orange-yellow solid powder for further use. Different N,S-CQDs were fabricated by adjusting the amounts of 3-aminothiophenol (0.01 g, 0.03 g, 0.05 g, 0.08 g and 0.1 g) with the same synthetic procedures.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM), high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra, zeta potential, UV–Vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra and fluorescence lifetimes were used to characterize N,S-CQDs. The relevant instrument parameters are consistent with our published articles [12,25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) measurement of N,S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
QY was determined according to a determined method [12], which contrasts the integrated fluorescence intensities and optical densities between the determinand and the reference. Herein, fluorescein (0.1 M dissolved in EtOH with the QY of 79%) was chosen as the reference, the QY of N,S-CQDs was determined by the following equation (1):
(1)
Q
Y D
= Q
Y R
Gra
d D
/ G r a
d R
η D 2
/
η R 2
Where the subscripts of D and R show N,S-CQDs and fluorescein, respectively. Grad and η is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensities against optical densities and the refractive index (ηD = 1.33 and ηR = 1.33), respectively. The optical intensity in the 10 mm path-length fluorescence cuvette was kept under 0.10 at the excitation wavelength of 476 nm to minimize the self-absorption effect.

To accessing the selectivity of N,S-CQDs, the final concentration of 0.5 mM of AZM, seventeen kinds of amino acids (Asp, Glu, Thr, Val, Tyr, Pro, Gln, Phe, Asn, Cys, Ala, Ile, Leu, Gly, Ser, Trp and Met), five kinds of vitamins (VB1, VB2, VB3, VB7 and VB9) and eighteen kinds of drugs (THI, CLA, GEN, DOX, VAN, LIN, SD, TIN, MET, ORN, AMX, UA, PEN, AC, CA, PG, CH and MH) were mixed with 380 μL N,S-CQDs (0.1 mg/mL), respectively. The fluorescence intensities were obtained at λex/λem of 476 nm/528 nm, respectively. 20 μL of ultrapure water was mixed with 380 μL of N,S-CQDs (0.1 mg/mL) to measure the fluorescence intensity and used as a reference sample.

Three kinds of AZM dispersible tablets (YiLing, PuLeQi and XiPuLe) were purchased from the local pharmacy in Taiyuan of China. 0.3799 g, 0.0706 g and 0.5039 g the crushed YiLing, PuLeQi and XiPuLe tablets powder was weighted and mixed with 10 mL MeOH to form a homogeneous AZM tablet solution by ultrasonic treatment for 30 min, respectively. The AZM tablet solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to obtain the supernatant, which served as the mother liquor for further use. After the mother liquor of YiLing, PuLeQi and XiPuLe was diluted ten times, thirty times and thirty times, respectively, the contents of AZM in YiLing, PuLeQi and XiPuLe were determined by the constructed probe. The mother liquor was diluted to 0.5 μM by ultrapure water for further use. 200 μL of N,S-CQDs (1 mg/mL) was added to 2 mL of AZM tablet solution (0.5 μM) to prepare the actual sample solution. Then different concentrations of AZM standard solution (5 μM and 50 μM) were added into the actual sample solution to evaluate the recovery and reproducibility of the proposed method. Urine samples from two healthy adults were diluted 1000 times in ultrapure water to test the recovery and reproducibility of this fluorescent probe by adding different concentrations of AZM standard solution (5 μM, 50 μM and 60 μM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular toxicity experiment and cellular imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Human non-small cell lung cancer A549 and Rattus norvegicus adrenal pheochromocytoma PC12 as model cells to evaluate the cytotoxicity of N,S-CQDs, the experiment steps are as follows: Cells were inoculated into 96-microwell plates and kept at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h to ensure the adherence of cells. Then the cells were treated with different concentrations of N,S-CQDs for 24 h under the same condition. After removal of the supernatant, 90 μL of free DMEM and 10 μL of CCK-8 were added into every micro-well and then the plate was incubated in 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 40 min. Finally, the optical density (OD) of the mixture in each micro-well was determined at 450 nm by a SunRise microplate reader (Tecan Austria Gmbh, Grödig, Austria). The survival rates of A549 and PC12 were calculated referring the formula in our published works [12].

A549 and PC12 cells were inoculated onto the laser confocal dish and incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h. N,S-CQDs of 0.1 mg/mL and N,S-CQDs of 100 µg/mL with different concentration of AZM (0.1 mM, 0.2 mM or 0.4 mM) was respectively added into the dish, and then the dish was placed in the incubator for 8 h. Then A549 and PC12 cells were washed three times by DPBS to remove residual N,S-CQDs. Laser confocal microscopic imaging was performed on a ZEISS LSM 880 with an Airyscan super-resolution confocal microscopy (Germany) under the excitation at 488 nm. All the images were obtained under the constant settings.

The AZM content determined by an Agilent 1260 series (Boeblingen, Germany) HPLC consisting of a quaternary pump, an Agilent 1260 series variable wavelength detector (VWD), a Chem Station software and an Agilent C18 column (4.6 mm × 250 mm; 5 mm, Agilent Technologies Inc., California, USA). The mobile phase was composed of phosphate buffer and MeOH (30:70, v/v) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min with the column temperature of 40 °C. The phosphate buffer was obtained by dissolving 4.55 g KH2PO4 in 1000 mL ultrapure water, and then pH was adjusted to 7.5 by NaOH (0.1 M). The mobile phase was filtered through a 0.45 µm membrane filter and ultrasonically degassed prior to use. The chromatograms were obtained by monitoring the UV detector at 210 nm. The volume of injection was 20 µL and the analysis time was 50 min per sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of samples and calibration curves
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained mother liquor of YiLing, PuLeQi and XiPuLe in 2.6 was respectively diluted ten times, thirty times and thirty times in MeOH and then filtered through 0.45 µm syringe filter prior to HPLC analysis. Urine samples from two healthy adults were diluted 1000 times in MeOH and then filtered through 0.45 µm syringe filter prior to HPLC analysis. AZM stock solution (10 mM) was made with MeOH. Working standard solutions for calibration curves were prepared by diluting the stock solution with MeOH at eight different concentration levels (0.05 mM, 0.08 mM, 0.1 mM, 0.2 mM, 0.4 mM, 0.6 mM, 0.8 mM and 1.0 mM). All the stock and working solutions were kept at −18 °C in a freezer.

The one-step hydrothermal method can be used to prepare different types of N,S-CQDs by tuning the amounts of 3-aminothiophenol, which is depicted in Fig. S1. When the amounts of 3-aminothiophenol were 0.01 g, 0.03 g, 0.05 g, 0.07 g, 0.08 g and 0.1 g, the fluorescence QYs of N,S-CQDs are 9.60%, 8.64%, 11.70%, 11.96%, 11.17% and 7.11%, respectively. The highest fluorescence QY is 11.96% for N,S-CQDs prepared with 0.07 g 3-aminothiophenol and 10 mL EtOH under the reaction of 180 °C and 12 h. So this N,S-CQDs was chosen for further detailed research.

The morphology and size distribution of N,S-CQDs are characterized by AFM and TEM technologies. As depicted in Fig. 1 A, N,S-CQDs are evenly distributed onto silicon slice with the average height of 1.5 nm. The representative TEM image (Fig. 1B) indicates that the size range and average size of N,S-CQDs (the inset in Fig. 1B) are 2.25–6.25 nm and 4.25 ± 0.5 nm, respectively. And N,S-CQDs are uniformly distributed on the surface of copper grid with ultra-thin carbon thin and possess distinct crystal lattice of 0.24 nm (the inset in Fig. 1B), attributing to the d-spacing of the graphene (100) planes. These results demonstrate that N,S-CQDs are non-spherical structure, which is different from other reported CQDs [10] and makes N,S-CQDs have innovative applications (vide infra). Simultaneously, N,S-CQDs have excellent water solubility and dispersibility.

XPS and FTIR are important measures to analyze the surface structure and functional groups. There are five distinct peaks at 528.51, 396.82, 282.16, 229.31 and 165.27 eV, corresponding to O1s, N1s, C1s, S2s and S2p, respectively, which shown in Fig. 2 A. The inset in Fig. 2A indicates that the contents of C, N, S and O are calculated as 45.10%, 7.52%, 8.1% and 39.28%, respectively. In C1s XPS spectrogram (Fig. 2B), four peaks at 286.12, 285.38, 284.85 and 284.39 eV are related to CO, CN, CC and CS, respectively [26–28]. The N1s XPS spectrogram is fitted with three nitrogenous functional groups including graphitic-N, NC and pyrrolic-N, which correspond respectively with 401.26, 400.70 and 399.09 eV (Fig. 2C) [27,29]. As shown in Fig. 2D, S2p can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 168.45, 165.01 and 163.93 eV, which are designated as S2p,sat CS, S2p CS and S2p ½ CS, respectively [27,30–32]. The high-resolution O1s XPS spectrogram (Fig. 2E) can be divided into four peaks at 533.03, 532.27, 531.68 and 531.1 eV, representing CO, COH/COC, CNO and CO, respectively [27,30]. All XPS data manifest that N and S atoms have been resoundingly doped into N,S-CQDs. FTIR was devoted to study the structure of N,S-CQDs and is shown in Fig. 2F. In FTIR spectrum, four peaks at 3210–3400 cm−1, 2120 cm−1, 1634 cm−1 and 1396 cm−1 are attributed to nitrogen-containing groups NH [33], NN [12], CN [30] and CN [27], respectively. Sulfur-rich functional groups such as CS [25] and CCS [34] locate at 1240 cm−1 and two peaks of 885 and 511–740 cm−1, respectively. Other characteristic peaks at 3668 and 3210–3400 cm−1, 2902–2981 cm−1 and 1062 cm−1 are ascribed to OH, CH and CO, respectively [25,34]. Some characteristic peaks such as NH (3350 and 3436 cm−1), CN (1437 and 1472 cm−1) and CS (1265 cm−1) are found in the FTIR spectrum of 3-aminothiophenol, demonstrating that these nitrogen and sulfur-containing functional groups were derived from 3-aminothiophenol. Furthermore, new peaks including CH, NN, CN, CO and CCS only exist in the FTIR of N,S-CQDs, indicating that 3-aminothiophenol was completely carbonized to form N,S-CQDs. These results from FTIR were in good accordance with the XPS analysis. All results fully demonstrate that N,S-CQDs have been functionalized by abundant functional groups including NH, NN, CN, CN, CO, CS and CCS, ensuring the good water solubility, excellent optical performance and remarkable sensing property (vide infra).

Optical property is one of the most important features for CQDs, which can be characterized by absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectrum and quantum yield (QY). As shown in Fig. 3 A (red line), three strong absorption peaks at 215 nm, 254 nm and 353 nm are attributed to the π-π* transition of conjugate system, [35] the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 bond [27] and n-π* transition of the CO bond [34], respectively. The wide absorption peak at 430–520 nm is corresponded to the excited defect surface states induced by the N and S heteroatoms [12,25,28]. The green and blank lines in Fig. 3A indicate that the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of N,S-CQDs are 476 nm and 528 nm, respectively. N,S-CQDs can emit bright green fluorescence when 365 nm UV light was used to stimulate N,S-CQDs, while N,S-CQDs are extremely pale color under natural light, the obtained shooting figures are inserted in Fig. 3A. Furthermore, the emission spectra of N,S-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths were estimated and reveal in Fig. 3B. When the excitation wavelength of N,S-CQDs varies from 350 nm to 510 nm, the emission peaks of N,S-CQDs ranges from 525 nm to 537 nm. The small shift demonstrates that N,S-CQDs have excitation-independent fluorescence behavior, which may be attributed to the π* → π transitions of the graphitic structure of the carbon cores [12]. Finally, fluorescein as reference, the FQY of N,S-CQDs was measured and shown in Fig. S2. The FQY of N,S-CQDs was calculated as 11.96%, illustrating that N,S-CQDs have high luminous efficiency and potential applications in the field of sensing and imaging (vide infra).

The fluorescence stability of N,S-CQDs under different ionic strength, pH, Xe lamp irradiation and UV irradiation was investigated and is revealed in Fig. S3. When the concentration of NaCl varied from 0.0 M to 2.0 M, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs has almost no change (Fig. S3A), demonstrating that N,S-CQDs has good property of resisting salt effect and is suitable for cell imaging. pH is a vital parameter for evaluating the fluorescence stability of N,S-CQDs, the analysis results are displayed in Fig. S3B. When pH decreases from 10.0 to 4.0, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs gradually increases and then researches the highest value at pH 4.0–2.0. However, when pH is higher than 10.0, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs sustains a constant. So N,S-CQDs is sensitive to pH, which may be attributed to the changes of surface functional groups of N,S-CQDs under different pHs. Photostability of N,S-CQDs was studied by UV and Xe lamp irradiation for 180 min, respectively, which is shown in Fig. S3C and S3D. Continuous irradiation by UV and Xe lamp cannot cause any change in fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs, elucidating that N,S-CQDs has excellent stability against photobleaching. These results indicate that N,S-CQDs possess storing stability and can be used in sensing and imaging (vide infra).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive determination of AZM by N,S-CQDs and mechanism research
ce:section.ce:para: The most surprising discovery is that AZM can extremely quench the fluorescence of N,S-CQDs, so a fluorescent probe for the determination of AZM was constructed. The selectivity of the probe was firstly discussed. Some amino acids, vitamins and drugs were added into N,S-CQDs, the intensity of N,S-CQDs hardly changes at all (Fig. S4). AZM can strongly quench the fluorescence of N,S-CQDs, so N,S-CQDs can selectively identify AZM without any interference. N,S-CQDs is sensitive to pH, so the quenching effect of AZM to the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs in different solvent (ultrapure water and PBS at different pH) was estimated and is shown in Fig. 4 A. When 0.56 μM AZM was added into N,S-CQDs with different pH, the quenching rate is between 78.0% and 93.5%; while the quenching rate of that in ultrapure water is about 71.0%, so ultrapure water was chosen as the optimal solvent for further experiment. Furthermore, the titration experiment was used to investigate the sensitivity of the fluorescent probe to AZM detection. As depicted in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence strength of N,S-CQDs is decreased as the AZM concentration increases from 0.0 μM to 474 μM. The inserted image in Fig. 5 B is the trend chart of the whole titration experiment. Two linear ranges of 2.5–32.3 μM and 37.2–110 μM can be found in Fig. 4C and 4D, respectively. The linear equation and correlation coefficient R2 are (F0-F)/F0 = 0.000896[AZM] + 0.00449 and 0.9934 and (F0-F)/F0 = 0.00196[AZM] − 0.0404 and 0.9933, respectively, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N,S-CQDs in the absence and presence of AZM, respectively. [AZM] is the concentration of AZM. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated as 0.76 µM by the equation of LOD = 3σ/k (where σ and k are the relative standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs (n = 20) and the slope of the calibration graph, respectively), which is lower than some reported detection methods [36].

A comparison between the formerly reported analytical methods and this work for detecting of AZM is listed in Table 1 . The proposed fluorescence method here has much higher sensitivity than some advanced modern instrumental method HPLC-PDA [36]. Compared to the previously reported HPLC-PCD-FLD [7] and LLLME-LC [9], the constructed fluorescent probe achieved a wider range of linearity. The analytical performance of the fluorescent probe is not as outstanding as that of LC-MS/MS [1], but our constructed N,S-CQDs-based fluorescent probe provides advantages of low-cost, easy accessibility, stable operation function and shorten analysis time.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism for AZM determination was investigated in detail. Firstly, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of AZM, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N,S-CQDs were investigated and are shown in Fig. S5. No coverage can be found between the absorption spectrum of AZM and the fluorescence spectra of N,S-CQDs, so internal filtration effect (IFE) or FRET cannot occur between N,S-CQDs and AZM. Furthermore, the zeta potentials and fluorescence lifetimes of N,S-CQDs were determined to illustrate the fluorescence quenching mechanism. The zeta potentials of N,S-CQDs, AZM and the mixture of N,S-CQDs and AZM were tested and are shown in Fig. 5A. The zeta potential of N,S-CQDs is 16.2 mV with positive potential, while the zeta potential of AZM is −4.2 mV with negative potential. As we had expected, the zeta potential of the mixture of N,S-CQDs and AZM is 4.1 mV, which is situated between that of N,S-CQDs and AZM. This result demonstrates that an strong electrostatic interaction is existing between N,S-CQDs and AZM. We conclude that the fluorescence of N,S-CQDs may be related to the surface groups (NH2, HS, etc.) of N,S-CQDs. The electrostatic interaction between N,S-CQDs and AZM promotes the strong binding of AZM to the surface functional groups of N,S-CQDs, which may cause the changes of functional groups on the surface of N,S-CQDs and lead to fluorescence quenching. That is, the selectivity of N,S-CQDs to AZM is closely related to the surface functional groups of N,S-CQDs, which is confirmed by the selective experiments of two other CQDs against AZM. The results in Fig. S6 indicated that AZM cannot quench the fluorescence of N,P,Cl-CQDs (Fig. S6A) and N,S@CQDs (Fig. S6B), while the fluorescence of N,S-CQDs (Fig. S6C) can be markedly quenched by AZM, demonstrating that the specific surface structure and functional groups of N,S-CQDs determine the specific selectivity of N,S-CQDs to AZM. Electrostatic interaction not only helps N,S-CQDs and AZM get closer together, but also is conductive to effectively quench fluorescence of N,S-CQDs by AZM.

Static quenching and dynamic quenching are two common quenching mechanisms, which can be confirmed by fluorescence lifetime. The fluorescence lifetime of a fluorophore will not change in static quenching process while the fluorescence lifetime of a fluorophore will reduce in dynamic quenching process [18]. The fluorescence lifetimes of N,S-CQDs in the absence and presence of different concentration of AZM were assessed and are shown in Fig. 5B. The average lifetime of N,S-CQDs is 4.69 ns (χ2 = 1.064) with τ1 and τ2 of 1.1492 ns (63.54%) and 10.8721 ns (36.46%), respectively. When AZM with different concentrations (2.5, 7.5, 12.5, 22.4, 37.0, 52.0 and 67.0 μM) was added into N,S-CQDs, the lifetimes of the mixture of N,S-CQDs and AZM are between 4.64 ns and 4.86 ns (Table S1). The change rate of lifetime is below ±3.6%, indicating that the fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CQDs has a negligible effect by AZM under the high concentration (67.0 μM), corresponding to the static quenching process. In consequence, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N,S-CQDs by AZM may be attributed to a synergistic effect of electrostatic interaction and static quenching, not IFE or FRET (Fig. 5C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Qualitative analysis of AZM in living cells by N,S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity was firstly assessed for further using N,S-CQDs in living cells. A549 and PC12 as model cells, the different concentrations of N,S-CQDs (0, 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 μg/mL) were co-incubated with model cells for 24 h, and the results are depicted in Fig. 6 A. When the concentration of N,S-CQDs was 100 μg/mL, the viability of A549 and PC12 is 79.61% and 76.31%, respectively, illustrating that N,S-CQDs has lower cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility.

The non-toxic property ensures that N,S-CQDs can be used for cell imaging without a risk of cellular damage. As described the laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) images of bright field in Fig. 6B, A549 and PC12 cells with good cellular morphology further indicates the biological compatibility of N,S-CQDs. Under the λex/λem of 488 nm/490–650 nm, A549 and PC12 cells can emit bright green emission when they were co-incubated with N,S-CQDs (100 μg/mL) for 8 h. When A549 and PC12 cells were respectively co-incubated with the mixture of N,S-CQDs (100 μg/mL) and AZM (0.1 mM, 0.2 mM or 0.4 mM) for 8 h, the green emission from the cells was significantly decreased and even disappeared. Under the same observation conditions, AZM can weaken the fluorescence of N,S-CQDs in live cells, demonstrating that the constructed fluorescent probe can be used to qualitatively analyze AZM in complex cellular environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative determination of AZM in dosage forms by N,S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N,S-CQDs-based fluorescent probe was applied for quantitative detection of AZM in commercially available AZM dispersible tablets (YiLing, PuLeQi and XiPuLe) obtained from the local pharmacy (Taiyuan, Shanxi, China). To keep the concentration of AZM in dispersible tablets within the linear range, the mother liquor of YiLing, PuLeQi and XiPuLe were diluted ten times, thirty times and thirty times, respectively. Urine samples were collected from two healthy adults and diluted 1000 times with ultrapure water. As displayed in Table 2 A, the AZM contents in YiLing dispersible tablet, PuLeQi dispersible tablet, XiPuLe dispersible tablet, urine sample-1 and urine sample-2 determined by the constructed probe are 17.93 µM, 85.56 µM, 81.82 µM, 0.00 µM and 0.00 µM, respectively, with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) in the range of 1.92–3.73% (n = 3). The mother liquor was diluted to the concentration of AZM with 0.5 μM by ultrapure water for further use. Recovery test was used to evaluate the practicability of the proposed method, and AZM with various concentrations were spiked into the diluted drug and urine samples. As shown in Table 2B, the quantitative spike recoveries for the determination of AZM are 98.2–104.8% with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) between 0.04% and 3.46% (n = 3). These results reveal that the constructed probe can realize quantify AZM contents in commercial medicine and human urine with a high degree of accuracy and sensitivity.

In order to validate the accuracy of the proposed fluorescent probe, a HPLC-UV method, which has been widely used for AZM detection [3,4], was used to determine AZM in AZM dispersible tablets and human urine samples. The linear equation between the concentration of AZM and integral area was constructed with a linear range from 50 to 1000 µM for AZM detection (Fig. S7). In order to make the concentration of AZM in dispersible tablets within the linear range, the mother liquor of YiLing, PuLeQi and XiPuLe were diluted 10, 30 and 30 times, respectively. The analysis results are displayed in Table S2. The contents of AZM in YiLing dispersible tablet, PuLeQi dispersible tablet, XiPuLe dispersible tablet, urine sample-1 and urine sample-2 are calculated as 17.96 µM, 85.77 µM, 83.09 µM, 0.00 µM and 0.00 µM, respectively. A good agreement between the results obtained by our proposed fluorescence method and that acquired by the HPLC-UV method, which further demonstrate the accuracy of our proposed fluorescence method.

A simple, rapid and low-cost fluorescent probe was constructed for the sensitive detection of AZM based on the effective fluorescence quenching of N,S-CQDs fabricated by the hydrothermal treatment of 3-aminothiophenol. The constructed fluorescent probe reveals some excellent performances including easy to synthesis, high economy-effectiveness, good selectivity, remarkable sensitivity (0.76 µM), wide linear range (2.5–32.3 μM and 37.2–110 μM) and fine biocompatibility. The fluorescent probe was used to detect AZM in cells, tablets and human urine with fulfilling results. The recoveries varied from 98.2% to 104.8% with the RSDs < 3.46%. All findings obviously testify that the fluorescent probe has high potential for detecting AZM in complex cellular environment, commercially available medicines and human urine. Our proposed fluorescent probe is simple, low-cost and easy to operate, which can be expected to have a wide potential application in areas such as drug administration and medication guidance.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21705101), Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Project (No. 201903D121109) and Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 201801D121040 and 201901D211154) were used to support this work. Sincerely thank Dr. Ying Zuo of the Scientific Instrument Centre at Shanxi University for taking the AFM images.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119506.

ce:section.ce:para:

Interfaces 11 (2019) 2243-2253, https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.8b17128.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A rapid, developed and validated RP-HPLC method for determination of azithromycin
SN Appl. Sci.
